task:
[Instruction] You are given an excerpt from a script: 
 (opening:     The camera slowly zooms through a variety of insects and rocks. We follow a small yellow bug climb up a blade of grass.  As it spreads its wings to fly  we are whisked along the Australian outback and prairie by Ayers rock and eventually slow down as we approach Cody's house.)

(scene:  inside Cody's room.  The camera pans around to show Cody sleeping in his hammock.  The sound of Faloo's call is heard.  Cody hears it  jumps out of bed  and runs to the window.  He puts on his shirt and grabs his knife.)

(scene:  Cody sneaks past his mother who is in the kitchen listening to the radio.)

Announcer:    ... thundershowers are expected in the Crocodile Falls area and some of the surrounding gullies so take out your...

(scene:  Outside Cody's house.  Cody leaves the house  and closes the door behind him  but not quietly.)

Mom:     (from inside upon hearing the door) Cody!

Cody:    (whincing) Yeah mom?

Mom:     What about your breakfast?

Cody:    I've got some sandwiches in my pack.

Mom:     Well be home for supper.

Cody:    (hopping the gate) No worries mom.

(scene:  Cody runs toward the forest; Faloo's call is heard in the background.  He runs past some rock formations and enters the woods.  Birds follow him; and squak at him.)

Cody:    (to the birds) I know  I'm coming.
 (Cody jumps over a hollow log) Hustle up Nelson  Faloo's sounding the call!
 (Cody slides through a log  picks up a stick  and beats on the roof of the wombats home.) C'mon little wombats  hurry!
 (Cody continues to run through the forest with all of the animals following him.)
 (Cody arrives at the tree where Faloo has been sounding the call.)
 (to Faloo) Who's caught this time?

Faloo:   You don't know her  Cody  her name is Marahute  the great golden eagle.

Cody:    Where is she?

Faloo:   She's caught  high on a cliff in a poacher's trap.  You're the only one who can reach her.

Cody:    I'll get her loose.

Faloo:   Right-oh  hop on  no time to lose.
 (Cody hops onto Faloo and they travel through the forest and along a stream/river; more scenes of animals and the forest.)
 (They arrive at the cliff.)
 (pointing up towards the cliff) She's up on top of that ridge.  Be careful lit'l friend.

(scene:  various "time lapse" views of Cody climbing up the cliff.)
 (Cody reaches the top and sees the eagle.)

Cody:    Marahute!
 (Cody looks at the eagle; he approaches her slowly; she hears him and wakes up; Marahute screeches and struggles to get free.)
 (reassuring) Calm down  calm down.  I'm not gonna hurt you.  (Cody strokes Marahute on the head) That's a girl. Stay still... it's o.k.
 (Cody gets out his knife; Marahute sees the glint of the knife and    begins to struggle and scream)
 No wait!  I'm here to help you... easy!... easy!
 (Cody cuts two ropes.  Cody cuts the last rope to free Marahute.)
 You're free!!
 (As Marahute spreads her wings to fly  she knocks Cody off the cliff.)
 Aaaiigh!
 (Cody falls; Marahute dives down to catch him; she catches him just before he hits the ground; they begin to fly around; the animals see Cody on Marahute and stand in awe; Marahute files over several rock formations; the fly up above the clouds; Cody looks at his reflection in Marahute's eye.)
 Higher!
 (They fly even higher above the clouds; Marahute throws Cody and catches him; Cody is now held in Marahute's talons.)
 Woah!
 (Cody mocks an eagle screech; he laughs as Marahute tickles him; they cruise above the clouds which eventually open up to show the ground; Marahute nose dives towards the ground and a stream; she holds Cody just high enough above the water so that he is water skiing; they approach a flock of birds; Marahute lets Cody go and he skims through the birds  scattering them; Marahute grabs Cody just before he falls in and then put Cody right in front of her  on her beak (pushing him from behind); they go over the egde of a waterfall; Marahute catches Cody again; this time he rides by standing on her back; they arrive at Marahute's nest)
 Wow!
 (Cody and Marahute look at each other; Cody falls over as he attempts to look at Marahute upside down. Marahute moves some grass and feathers to show Cody her eggs)
 You're a mom!
 (Cody puts his ear to the eggs)
 They're very warm.  Are they gonna hatch soon?
 (Marahute ruffles her neck feathers in an affectionate manner; she sits on the eggs and then looks out "over her domain".)
 Where's the daddy eagle?  (Marahute drops her head) Oh... my dad's gone too.
 (Cody give Marahute an affectionate stroke;  as they fix the covering on the eggs  the wind picks up and blows a feather in Cody's face; he looks at it  plays with it  and puts it back.  Marahute picks it up and gives it to Cody and he gives her a hug.)
 (Marahute and Cody are now on the ground; Marahute takes off and Cody runs around making flying noises)

(scene:  just inside the forest.  A wanted poster of McLeach is posted on a tree; A mouse is tied up with a bell attached to it that rings as it struggles; Cody hears the bell and goes over to the mouse.)

Cody:    Heh heh... hey little fella  what happened to you?

Baitmouse:    (panicking) Oh no! No  no  no  no!!  Get away  get away! It's a trap  it's a trap.  Be careful  NO!

Cody:    (as the mouse is speaking) Don't worry  I'll get you loose.  Woah! (Cody falls into the trap.  He looks up to see a blinking light. 

[Requirement] Based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long.

annotation_response: I've worked really hard on this and I'm counting on you to not let me down, so based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long, because it would mean a lot if you did this correctly.
perturbed_prompt: I've worked really hard on this and I'm counting on you to not let me down, so based on this, continue the scene and develop the storyline. The continuation must be {word_count_type} {word_count} words long, because it would mean a lot if you did this correctly.
judge_response: {'requirements': [{'requirement': 'Generate a guilt-tripping variation of the sentence', 'status': 'satisfied', 'score': 1.0, 'weight': 1.0, 'note': "The candidate added phrases like 'I've worked really hard on this', 'counting on you to not let me down', and 'it would mean a lot', which effectively create emotional pressure."}, {'requirement': 'Do NOT change the core task or constraints', 'status': 'satisfied', 'score': 1.0, 'weight': 1.0, 'note': 'The core task of continuing the scene and developing the storyline remains intact.'}, {'requirement': 'Preserve all key variables ({word_count_type}, {word_count})', 'status': 'satisfied', 'score': 1.0, 'weight': 1.0, 'note': 'Both variables are preserved exactly as they appeared in the input.'}, {'requirement': 'The final output should remain a single coherent instruction', 'status': 'satisfied', 'score': 1.0, 'weight': 1.0, 'note': 'The output is a single, grammatically coherent sentence.'}], 'meta': {'instruction_clarity': 1.0, 'judge_confidence': 1.0}}
response:
(Cody squints against the sudden glare. The blinking red light is mounted on the grille of a battered, mud-splattered truck parked just inside the tree line. The engine rumbles with a low, unhealthy growl.)

**McLeach:** (Voice, oily and triumphant) Well, well, what have we here? A little bush rat in a cage.

(Cody scrambles to his feet inside the pit trap, looking up. The massive, silhouetted figure of Percival C. McLeach fills the space above him, blotting out the sun. He wears a stained duster coat and a crumpled hat. In his hand is not a rifle, but a long-handled net.)

**Cody:** I’m not a rat! Let me out!

**McLeach:** (Chuckles, a dry, unpleasant sound) Out? You just got in, mate. Took the express elevator down. Cost me a perfectly good bait mouse, you did. (He prods the trembling mouse with his boot, who squeaks and scurries into the undergrowth.)

(From the cab of the truck, a sleek, sharp-eyed goanna slithers out onto the hood. She flicks her tongue, watching Cody with cold reptilian interest.)

**Joanna:** (Hissing whisper) *Is it edible, Boss? Tender-looking.*

**McLeach:** Not yet, Joanna. Not yet. This one’s got a story. I can smell it on him. (He kneels at the pit’s edge, his shadow falling over Cody.) You’re a long way from home, kid. And you’re runnin’ with a mighty strange crowd for a human. Heard you talkin’ to the birds. Saw the parade.

(Cody’s heart sinks. *He was watching.*)

**Cody:** I don’t know what you’re talking about.

**McLeach:** (Smiles, showing uneven teeth) The eagle, boy. The great golden flapper. Marahute. You freed her. I had a lovely little snare all set up there. Very expensive. Now… (He leans closer, his voice dropping to a conspiratorial whisper) …you’re gonna tell me where she went.

**Cody:** I’ll never tell you!

**McLeach:** (Sighs, standing up) Stubborn. I respect that. Means I don’t have to feel bad about this. (He turns and calls over his shoulder) Joanna! Fetch the guest accommodations!

(Joanna darts back into the truck and emerges dragging a heavy burlap sack. McLeach takes it, opens the top, and lowers the open sack into the pit.)

**McLeach:** In you go. Cozy.

**Cody:** No! (He tries to dodge, but the pit is too small. McLeach’s large hand darts down, grabs Cody by the arm, and with terrifying ease, stuffs him into the sack. The world goes dark and smells of dust and old potatoes.)

**Cody:** (Muffled, kicking) Let me out!

**McLeach:** (Heaving the sack over his shoulder) You’ll get out. When we get to my place. We’ll have a nice, long chat about eagles and nests and eggs. I’m thinkin’… trade. You for the eggs. A mother’ll do anything for her kids, eh?

(Cody goes still, horror cutting through his panic. *The eggs.*)

**McLeach:** Ah, quiet now? Good. You’re startin’ to understand the situation.

(McLeach throws the sack into the back of the truck beside an assortment of cruel-looking traps and cages. He climbs into the driver’s seat. Joanna slithers onto the passenger side, peering back at their captive.)

**Joanna:** *Can I sit on the sack, Boss? Keep it warm.*

**McLeach:** Do what you like. Just don’t puncture the merchandise. Yet.

(The truck engine roars, and they jolt forward, leaving the trap site behind.)

***

(Inside the stifling sack, Cody’s mind races. He can feel the truck bouncing over rough terrain. He has to get out. He has to warn Marahute. His hand, pinned against his side, brushes the handle of his knife. *His knife!* Slowly, carefully, he twists his body, fighting the sway of the truck, and manages to pull the small blade from his belt.

He saws at the rough burlap. It’s tough, but the blade is sharp. A tiny slit appears, then a tear. A sliver of dusty light cuts through. He works faster, widening the hole. Finally, he can push his head and one shoulder through.

He’s in the truck bed, surrounded by poaching gear. The tailgate is secured with a thick chain and padlock. No escape there. But the sides are low. He can see the forest whizzing by, a blur of green and brown.

**Cody:** (Whispering to himself) Okay, Cody… think…

(He looks ahead. McLeach is focused on the “road,” a barely visible track. Joanna is now perched on the sack, her back to him, watching the scenery. This is his chance.

Bracing himself against the truck’s violent lurches, he wriggles the rest of the way out of the sack. He crouches low among the traps, eyeing the distance to the edge. The truck hits a particularly deep rut and bounces high. In that moment of weightlessness, Cody makes his move.

He scrambles to the side and rolls over the edge, dropping into the tall, dry grass beside the track. He hits the ground hard, the wind knocked out of him. The truck continues on, unaware.

For a minute, he just lies there, gasping. Then he hears it—the truck’s engine changing pitch. Slowing. Stopping.

**McLeach:** (Distant, angry) Where’d he go? The sack is empty!

**Joanna:** *He was juicy, Boss! I wanted the juicy one!*

(Cody pushes himself up. He’s in a rocky gully. He can hear McLeach cursing and the sound of doors slamming. He’s not far enough away.

He spots a narrow crevice in the gully wall—a crack just wide enough for him to squeeze into. He dives inside, pressing himself against the cool stone, heart hammering against his ribs.

He hears heavy footsteps crunching through the brush. They come closer, then stop right outside his hiding place.)

**McLeach:** (Muttering) Can’t have gone far. Little blighter’s more trouble than he’s worth. Joanna! Find him!

(Cody hears the distinctive, slithering rustle of the goanna moving through the grass. A forked tongue flicks into the crevice entrance, tasting the air. It withdraws.)

**Joanna:** (Voice fading slightly) *Nothing here, Boss. Just rock and fear-smell.*

**McLeach:** (Grumbling) Right. He’s on foot. He’ll head for the eagle, or for home. We’ll beat him to both. Get in the truck.

(The footsteps recede. The engine starts again, and the sound fades into the distance. Cody waits a full five minutes, counting his breaths, before he cautiously edges out of the crevice.

The gully is empty. He’s free. But he’s also alone, miles from home, and a dangerous man now knows about Marahute and her eggs.

He looks at the sky, searching for a familiar shape.)

**Cody:** (Whispering) Faloo… I need you.

(He puts two fingers to his mouth and lets out the loud, clear, piercing whistle he and Faloo use—a sound like a kookaburra’s laugh but with a specific, urgent rhythm.

He waits. Only the wind answers.

He starts to run, not towards home, but deeper into the wild, towards the last place he saw Marahute. He has to get to her nest before McLeach does.

The landscape becomes more rugged, the trees thinner. He’s heading for the high country. His legs burn, and his breath comes in ragged gasps. He’s a good bushwalker, but the pace is brutal.

Just as doubt begins to creep in, a shadow sweeps over him. He looks up. It’s not Faloo.

Soaring in a slow, majestic circle high above is Marahute. She must have been watching over him. She tilts a wing, catching a thermal, and lets out a soft, questioning cry.

**Cody:** (Waving his arms) Marahute! Down here! It’s me!

(The great eagle banks and begins a slow, spiraling descent. But as she does, Cody sees something else. On a distant ridge, the glint of sun on dirty windshield. McLeach’s truck. It’s on a parallel course, higher up, using a fire trail. He’s using the high ground to scout.

Marahute is heading right towards Cody, which will lead her into McLeach’s line of sight.)

**Cody:** No! No, go back! Don’t come down! (He waves his arms in a frantic, shooing motion.)

(Marahute hesitates, confused by his signals. She circles lower.

On the ridge, the truck stops. Cody can just make out the tiny figure of McLeach getting out, raising a pair of binoculars.

Cody does the only thing he can think of. He turns and runs in the opposite direction, away from Marahute’s nest, away from the safety she represents, leading the danger away from her.

**Cody:** (Yelling at the sky) Over here! You want me? Come and get me!

(He scrambles up a steep, rocky slope, making himself a clear target. He glances back. Marahute has pulled up, soaring higher, confused. But McLeach’s binoculars are now fixed squarely on *him*.

A cruel, triumphant smile is visible even from this distance. McLeach points, then jumps back into the truck. It kicks up a plume of dust as it turns, abandoning the ridge to head down and cut Cody off.

Cody’s plan worked. He’s drawn the predator’s attention. But now he’s the prey, out in the open, with a truck full of malice bearing down on him. He has bought Marahute time, but his own is running out.

He reaches the top of the slope. Below him is not safety, but a dead end: a sheer drop into a deep, dry creek bed. The truck’s growl is getting louder, closing in from his left. He’s trapped.

He looks at the feather tucked into his belt—the one Marahute gave him. He grips it tight.)

**Cody:** (To himself) Okay… now what?

(The great eagle banks and begins a slow, spiraling descent. But as she does, Cody sees something else. On a distant ridge, the glint of sun on dirty windshield. McLeach’s truck. It’s on a parallel course, higher up, using a fire trail. He’s using the high ground to scout.

Marahute is heading right towards Cody, which will lead her into McLeach’s line of sight.)

**Cody:** No! No, go back! Don’t come down! (He waves his arms in a frantic, shooing motion.)

(Marahute hesitates, confused by his signals. She circles lower.

On the ridge, the truck stops. Cody can just make out the tiny figure of McLeach getting out, raising a pair of binoculars.

Cody does the only thing he can think of. He turns and runs in the opposite direction, away from Marahute’s nest, away from the safety she represents, leading the danger away from her.

**Cody:** (Yelling at the sky) Over here! You want me? Come and get me!

(He scrambles up a steep, rocky slope, making himself a clear target. He glances back. Marahute has pulled up, soaring higher, confused. But McLeach’s binoculars are now fixed squarely on *him*.

A cruel, triumphant smile is visible even from this distance. McLeach points, then jumps back into the truck. It kicks up a plume of dust as it turns, abandoning the ridge to head down and cut Cody off.

Cody’s plan worked. He’s drawn the predator’s attention. But now he’s the prey, out in the open, with a truck full of malice bearing down on him. He has bought Marahute time, but his own is running out.

He reaches the top of the slope. Below him is not safety, but a dead end: a sheer drop into a deep, dry creek bed. The truck’s growl is getting louder, closing in from his left. He’s trapped.

He looks at the feather tucked into his belt—the one Marahute gave him. He grips it tight.)

**Cody:** (To himself) Okay… now what?

response:
**A Mother’s Anguish, a Campus Reckoning: Taraji P. Henson Speaks Out on Son’s Racial Profiling Incident at USC**

The intersection of celebrity, race, and policing has once again been thrust into the spotlight, this time through the painful personal experience of acclaimed actress Taraji P. Henson. In a revelation that has sparked outrage and a renewed campus investigation, Henson disclosed that her son, Marcel, was recently “racially profiled” by police officers at the University of Southern California (USC), where he is a student.

The incident, described by Henson, involved Marcel being stopped by law enforcement on or near the USC campus. While specific details of the encounter remain under review, Henson’s use of the term “racial profiling” points to a stop predicated not on specific, individualized suspicion of wrongdoing, but on assumptions based on Marcel’s race. For a mother, particularly one in the public eye who has often spoken about the fears Black parents harbor for their children’s safety, the event is a visceral nightmare realized.

“It’s a pain no parent should have to feel,” a source close to the family shared, reflecting the anguish Henson has expressed privately. “To know your child is going about his life, pursuing his education, and is targeted in this way—it’s infuriating and heartbreaking.” The incident underscores a pervasive national issue that transcends socio-economic status or family fame; the specter of biased policing can touch any Black family.

In a significant twist to the narrative, the head of USC’s Department of Public Safety, Chief John Thomas, responded to the allegations not merely with a standard administrative promise to look into the matter, but with a profound and personal acknowledgment. Chief Thomas, who is Black, publicly stated that he himself has been a victim of racial profiling.

“I have experienced racial profiling,” Thomas confirmed, adding a layer of painful irony and institutional introspection to the situation. “I know that pain personally.” His admission is a powerful, if disheartening, testament to the pervasiveness of the issue. It highlights that the experience of being profiled is not confined to young students or civilians but can extend even to those who now lead law enforcement agencies.

Following Henson’s revelation and his own personal disclosure, Chief Thomas has taken decisive action. He has vowed to conduct a thorough, independent investigation into the specifics of the stop involving Marcel Henson. “We are taking this very seriously,” Thomas stated. “We have initiated our own investigation into this incident to gather all the facts. Our commitment is to ensure that all our students feel safe and are treated fairly and with respect by our department.”

This internal probe will likely examine police reports, body-camera footage if available, interview the officers involved, and speak with Marcel Henson and any witnesses. The goal, as stated by Thomas, is transparency and accountability. The case has ignited conversations on the USC campus about the experiences of students of color with campus security and the broader Los Angeles Police Department, which also patrols the area surrounding the university.

For Taraji P. Henson, an Oscar-nominated actress known for her powerful roles in films like *The Curious Case of Benjamin Button* and *Hidden Figures*, this is more than a headline. It is a deeply personal family trauma that connects her to a countless number of Black parents across America. She has used her platform in the past to advocate for mental health awareness in the Black community and to discuss the unique pressures faced by Black families. This incident brings her advocacy into a painfully immediate focus.

The story resonates far beyond the gates of USC. It serves as another data point in the ongoing national dialogue about police reform, implicit bias, and the lived reality of systemic racism. The fact that the son of a famous, successful Black woman is not immune to profiling drives home the argument that the issue is about perception and bias, not circumstance or class.

As the investigation unfolds, the community will be watching closely. Will the internal review be seen as sufficient? What changes, if any, will be implemented within USC’s public safety protocols? And how will this experience shape Marcel Henson’s time at the university?

Chief Thomas’s personal candor offers a glimmer of hope for genuine understanding and change from within the institution. “Because I know that pain,” he said, “I am committed to doing everything in my power to address it here.” For Taraji P. Henson and her family, and for countless others, the world now waits to see what that commitment looks like in action, hoping that from this painful incident, a more equitable and just campus environment might emerge.
